evaluation_inputs.system_description: The Dynamic Map is a complex information system designed for urban traffic optimization. It consists of spatial databases storing static and dynamic data (road infrastructure, traffic conditions, events) and software modules for data collection from various sensors (video, acoustic, GPS), interpretation, and provision to clients (visualization, route planning, traffic control).

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: Generic architectural views (ArchiMate) were available but lacked constructs to describe scenario influence on components or capture specific design decisions. Some specifications were missing or decisions had not yet been made.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: What is the platform for this component (e.g., PostgreSQL, Linux, IIS)?
Is the component logic fixed or customizable?
What is the query granularity (bulk, low, medium, high)?
What communication type is used (synchronous, asynchronous)?

hardware_view_description: Data originates from a network of fixed sensors (video detectors, acoustic sensors, inductive loops)
Data includes on-board GPS receivers installed in vehicles
End-user interaction via network or mobile interfaces
runtime_constraints: Real-time performance requirements for traffic analysis
High volume of data feeds from sensors
Interoperability with various sensor types and client modules

questions: How are transactions distributed across the distinct repositories (Static, Traffic, Event)?
How are ontology definitions synchronized with database dictionaries?
Is the query granularity optimized for bulk updates vs. individual reads?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: New sensor types and sensor processing software will be integrated in <6 days>
Dynamic Map will be capable of accepting feeds from up to 1000 sensors occurring every <60> sec
Dynamic Map will be capable of processing up to <200> read queries per second
New map features can be introduced and made discoverable with built-in functionality
Updating of data defining the road network can cause a loss of at most 10 values of a single monitoring parameter
Sensor failures will be detected in a configurable period ranging from 3 to 20 minutes
New event types and corresponding customizable detection routines can be introduced in 1 day
System will be able to accommodate and interpret location data from 5000 GPS tracker units updated every 5 sec
A Dynamic Map component can be ported to another platform within 20 days

business_goals: Optimize road traffic and provide tools for efficient traffic control
Enable fast response to road accidents or threats using real-time analysis
Provide efficient route planning and optimization based on dynamic traffic data
Integrate existing and new sensor infrastructure seamlessly
technical_constraints: Need for interoperability with diverse subsystems
architectural_drivers: Modifiability (Integration of new sensors and event types)

architectural_approaches.description: The system is centered around spatial databases (repositories) for static and dynamic data, with software modules for data collection, interpretation, and client provision.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Centralized data repositories allow efficient querying and real-time analysis for traffic optimization.
Modular design facilitates integration of new sensor types and event detection routines.
Structured data storage and configurable failure detection enhance system stability.

children.refinement: Real-time response for traffic optimization and incident alerts
children.scenario_description: During normal operations, the system processes incoming sensor data and generates traffic optimization suggestions or incident alerts within 2 seconds.

children.refinement: High throughput for sensor data ingestion
children.scenario_description: During peak traffic, the system ingests and stores data from thousands of heterogeneous sensors without data loss, utilizing buffering and scalable storage mechanisms.
A new type of traffic sensor is deployed. The system is modified to support data collection and interpretation from this sensor within one person-week of effort.

children.refinement: Addition of new event detection algorithms
children.scenario_description: A new algorithm for detecting a specific type of road hazard is developed. The system is updated to incorporate this algorithm without disrupting existing event detection services.
The core traffic optimization and control functions, supported by redundant components and failover mechanisms, maintain >99.9% uptime over a quarter.
If a regional data collection module fails, the system continues to provide traffic optimization for other regions and logs the failure for operator attention.

children.refinement: Efficient operator workflow for incident management
children.scenario_description: A traffic control operator can acknowledge a system-generated incident alert, view relevant sensor data, and deploy a response plan within 30 seconds.
The system's client provision modules are deployed and function correctly on both Windows and Linux server environments without major code changes.

scenarios.scenario_text: During normal operations, the system processes incoming sensor data and generates traffic optimization suggestions or incident alerts within 2 seconds.
During peak traffic, the system ingests and stores data from thousands of heterogeneous sensors without data loss, utilizing buffering and scalable storage mechanisms.
A new type of traffic sensor is deployed. The system is modified to support data collection and interpretation from this sensor within one person-week of effort.
A new algorithm for detecting a specific type of road hazard is developed. The system is updated to incorporate this algorithm without disrupting existing event detection services.
The core traffic optimization and control functions, supported by redundant components and failover mechanisms, maintain >99.9% uptime over a quarter.
If a regional data collection module fails, the system continues to provide traffic optimization for other regions and logs the failure for operator attention.
A traffic control operator can acknowledge a system-generated incident alert, view relevant sensor data, and deploy a response plan within 30 seconds.
The system's client provision modules are deployed and function correctly on both Windows and Linux server environments without major code changes.

scenario_reference.text: During normal operations, the system processes incoming sensor data and generates traffic optimization suggestions or incident alerts within 2 seconds.

analysis_records.response_measure: Generate traffic optimization suggestions or incident alerts within 2 seconds
architectural_decisions: Data-Centric / Repository Style with centralized spatial databases
Modular software components for data collection, interpretation, and client provision
analysis_records.reasoning: The centralized repository pattern can reduce data access latency for processing modules. However, without quantitative data on network latency, processing speeds, database query performance, and message volumes, it is impossible to verify if the 2-second target is achievable or to identify bottlenecks.

risks.description: Insufficient data to validate performance targets. The 2-second response time goal cannot be assessed without parameters like network bandwidth, message processing rates, and database latency.
risks.rationale: Critical quantitative parameters (requested by the Architect) are unavailable, preventing any latency calculation or bottleneck analysis.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Network bandwidth between sensors and repository
